skill.name: bodhi-sdk-react-integration
skill.description: Guide React and Vite projects through bodhi-js-sdk setup, OAuth configuration, local LLM chat integration, GitHub Pages deployment, and troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Add Bodhi local LLM chat support to React and Vite applications.
skill.author: BodhiSearch

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily overcounted Markdown code fences as Ruby backtick execution and marked documentation URLs as risky network behavior. Human review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill can run npm/npx commands and includes troubleshooting guidance that may expose OAuth token state in browser storage or console logs.

medium_findings.title: OAuth Token State May Be Exposed During Troubleshooting
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting guide recommends logging authentication state and reading localStorage values that may contain access tokens. This is legitimate debugging guidance, but users could accidentally share sensitive token data in logs or issue reports.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show auth state containing an accessToken and localStorage reads for Bodhi auth keys. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates those values, so the risk is exposure through debugging rather than malicious collection.

medium_findings.title: Permitted npm and npx Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash execution for npm and npx and instructs users to install packages, run dev servers, build apps, and preview deployments. These commands are expected for a React integration skill, but they execute project scripts and dependency lifecycle hooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and setup guides clearly authorize npm and npx usage. The commands are normal for this task, but npm scripts can execute arbitrary code from the target project or dependencies.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby backtick executions, but reviewed locations are Markdown fenced code blocks containing TypeScript, TSX, shell snippets, or prose. No Ruby backtick execution pattern was confirmed in executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review showed Markdown code fences and inline documentation, not Ruby code execution. The files are documentation-only, although some examples instruct users to run legitimate shell commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Expected Services
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs identify Bodhi developer, OAuth, GitHub Pages, Vite, and localhost endpoints needed for setup and troubleshooting. They are not evidence of covert network exfiltration, but users should verify service domains before entering credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as configuration endpoints for the documented SDK integration. I found no encoded payloads, hidden destinations, or instructions to send secrets to unrelated services.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Public Client Configuration
low_findings.description: The skill documents Vite environment variables and GitHub Actions secrets for OAuth client IDs and redirect URIs. These examples do not contain real secrets, but users should avoid committing production-specific values when their policy treats client IDs as sensitive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder client IDs and standard Vite public variables. OAuth client IDs are often public identifiers, but deployment teams may still classify them as sensitive configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access for Package Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to npm and npx commands. This supports the integration workflow but can run package lifecycle scripts in the user project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools front matter directly permits Bash(npm:*) and Bash(npx:*). This is a real execution capability, even though it is scoped to package tooling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Storage Token Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation tells users to inspect localStorage entries related to Bodhi authentication. This can reveal token material if copied into logs or support tickets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly read Bodhi auth keys from localStorage. The pattern is explainable for debugging, but it handles sensitive browser state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Localhost Network Diagnostics
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting guide recommends curl checks against the local Bodhi backend. This is expected for connectivity diagnostics and is limited to localhost endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit curl calls to localhost. They are not malicious, but they are external command and network access patterns that deserve disclosure.

content.user_title: Integrate Bodhi SDK Into React Apps
content.value_statement: React developers often need a clear path from a Vite app to local LLM chat with OAuth. This skill provides setup steps, code patterns, deployment guidance, and troubleshooting for bodhi-js-sdk.
actual_capabilities: Installs and configures @bodhiapp/bodhi-js-react in React and Vite projects.
Adds BodhiProvider setup and useBodhi hook patterns for chat interfaces.
Explains OAuth client registration for development and production environments.
Provides streaming chat examples using OpenAI-compatible completion APIs.
Covers GitHub Pages base path, callback, and deployment configuration.
Troubleshoots extension detection, local backend connection, auth, and model loading issues.
limitations: Focused on React and Vite projects, not other frontend frameworks.
Requires the Bodhi App backend and optional browser extension to be available.
Does not create OAuth clients automatically in the Bodhi developer portal.
Some troubleshooting steps inspect browser storage and should avoid shared token logs.

use_cases.title: Add local LLM chat to a React app
use_cases.description: Set up the Bodhi provider, create a chat component, load models, and stream responses in a Vite application.

use_cases.title: Prepare OAuth for production deployment
use_cases.description: Separate development and production client IDs, configure redirect URIs, and pass Vite environment variables through deployment workflows.

use_cases.title: Debug Bodhi integration failures
use_cases.description: Check extension status, backend reachability, authentication state, model loading, and streaming behavior with targeted troubleshooting steps.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing React and Vite app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Integrate bodhi-js-sdk into my React and Vite app. Add the provider, a simple chat component, and clear setup notes.

prompt_templates.title: Configure OAuth Environments
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure Bodhi OAuth for dev and production. Include redirect URI checks, Vite environment variables, and safe deployment notes.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy to GitHub Pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare my Bodhi React app for GitHub Pages. Fix base path handling, callback routing, and the build workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a Broken Integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced debugging across auth, extension, and backend state.
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose why my Bodhi React integration is not authenticating or loading models. Check provider configuration, backend reachability, and browser storage safely.

output_examples.input: Add Bodhi to my Vite React project.
output_examples.output: A concise integration plan with package installation, provider placement, chat component changes, and verification steps.
A deployment checklist covering Vite base path, callback path, redirect URI registration, and static routing behavior.

output_examples.input: Login works once, then the app loses authentication.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting sequence for browser storage access, base path mismatches, token refresh, and safe log redaction.

best_practices: Use separate OAuth clients for development and production environments.
Redact token values and auth state before sharing console logs.
Verify package scripts and dependency changes before running npm or npx commands.
anti_patterns: Do not commit real deployment configuration when your team treats client IDs as sensitive.
Do not paste localStorage auth values into public issues or support channels.
Do not reuse localhost redirect URIs for production OAuth clients.

faq.question: What projects does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports React applications built with Vite and the @bodhiapp/bodhi-js-react package.

faq.question: Does it require the Bodhi Browser extension?
faq.answer: The extension is preferred, but the SDK can use direct mode when the local backend is available.

faq.question: Can it deploy my app to GitHub Pages?
faq.answer: It provides configuration guidance for GitHub Pages, including base paths, callback handling, and workflow notes.

faq.question: Does it manage OAuth client registration?
faq.answer: No. It guides you through registration, but you create clients in the Bodhi developer portal.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it can run npm commands and includes debugging steps that may expose token state.

faq.question: How should I use troubleshooting logs safely?
faq.answer: Share only redacted logs. Remove access tokens, client identifiers, user data, and localStorage values first.
